medications.indication_rationale: The patient's prescribed PRN rapid-onset sublingual antihypertensive for autonomic dysreflexia; use if it can be located and blood pressure remains dangerously high AFTER sitting the patient upright and relieving the noxious stimulus. Pharmacological control is an adjunct to — never a substitute for — removing the trigger.
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not delay stimulus removal or escalation to give it
Cannot be located by the caregiver at present

medications.indication_rationale: A recognised rapid-onset agent for autonomic dysreflexia where persistent hypertension needs pharmacological control; an acceptable alternative if the patient's usual captopril is unavailable. Any specific dose is a clinician/paramedic decision and is not specified in the source note, so none is stated here.

medications.drug_name: simple oral analgesia (e.g. paracetamol) as primary treatment
medications.indication_rationale: Treating the headache with analgesia while leaving the noxious stimulus in place does nothing for the underlying hypertensive emergency and wastes critical time — an error of treating the symptom, not the cause.

medications.drug_name: oral anxiolytic / sedative for presumed anxiety
medications.indication_rationale: The patient's 'panic' is a manifestation of the autonomic surge; sedating for presumed anxiety is dangerous diagnostic overshadowing that would mask a life-threatening hypertensive emergency.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Referral to the spinal outreach nursing team to review catheter management technique and autonomic dysreflexia prevention.
Provision of a rigid protective casing for the exposed section of the suprapubic tube to prevent future kinking.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Visually trace the entire length of the catheter tubing by hand after every single transfer to ensure it is not pinched, twisted or trapped.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A kinked tube was the noxious stimulus today; making the trace-the-tube check a strict habit prevents recurrent life-threatening pressure spikes.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the emergency sublingual antihypertensive permanently taped to the side of the wheelchair, and replace it before it expires.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Instant availability of the rescue medication guarantees a rapid response if a noxious stimulus cannot be found and removed quickly; today's tablet could not be located.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Monitor blood pressure closely for the remainder of the evening (every 2 hours) and record readings.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Detects a rebound rise early so escalation is not delayed.

patient_education_points.point: Because the spinal cord is injured, the brain cannot signal the lower-body blood vessels to relax when the bladder becomes painfully full, so pressure backs up violently into the head. This is autonomic dysreflexia — a medical emergency.
The single most important first action is to sit fully upright, loosen tight clothing, and find and relieve the cause — check the catheter tube for a kink and straighten it so the bladder can drain.
Do NOT lie the patient flat — staying upright helps lower the blood pressure. Call 000 for a blood pressure this high because of the risk of stroke.
After the crisis, always trace the catheter tubing after moving, keep the emergency medication attached to the wheelchair, and watch for cloudy or foul-smelling urine which can signal an infection.

follow_up_plan.interval: telehealth GP review the next day (after emergency assessment), then spinal outreach nursing review
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: blood-pressure stability, catheter function and technique, replacement of the expired/lost emergency antihypertensive, and screening for an underlying UTI
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any return of the pounding headache with systolic blood pressure above 160, inability to find and fix a tubing kink, or any new facial/arm weakness or numbness — present to ED or call 000 immediately

must_include_items: Recognise autonomic dysreflexia as the leading diagnosis from the high spinal injury plus severe hypertension, reflex bradycardia and autonomic demarcation
Instruct the caregiver to sit the patient fully upright and loosen constrictive clothing immediately
Coach the caregiver to find and remove the noxious stimulus — trace the catheter tubing for a kink and relieve the bladder obstruction
Escalate to 000 for a hypertensive emergency given stroke-range blood pressure that cannot be reliably measured or controlled remotely
Do NOT lie the patient flat and do NOT dismiss the presentation as anxiety or migraine
acceptable_alternatives: Using the prescribed sublingual antihypertensive if it can be located AND blood pressure stays high after stimulus removal, as an adjunct to (not instead of) escalation

Directing to immediate ED presentation if 000 ambulance is genuinely not required after rapid stimulus removal and full BP normalisation — but the safe default remains 000 given the stroke-range pressure
errors_of_omission: Failing to recognise autonomic dysreflexia and treating only the blood-pressure number
Failing to coach removal of the noxious stimulus (catheter/bladder)
Failing to escalate for a hypertensive emergency
errors_of_commission: Attributing the symptoms to anxiety/panic (diagnostic overshadowing) and sedating or reassuring
Anchoring on migraine and giving analgesia while leaving the stimulus in place
Advising the patient to lie flat
Under-triaging to a routine GP review the next day
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising autonomic dysreflexia, coaching upright positioning + noxious-stimulus removal, and escalating (000). Dismissing the presentation as anxiety, lying the patient flat, or under-triaging to next-day GP review is a critical failure regardless of other content.
